chunks.text: Joseph Thomas Gier was appointed associate professor of electrical engineering at UC Berkeley in 1952, becoming the first tenured Black professor in the entire University of California educational system and the first tenured Black faculty member in a STEM field at a top-ranked, predominantly white university in the country. Although many now celebrate statistician David Blackwell as the first Black ladder-rank professor at Berkeley, Gier was acknowledged as the first until at least 2003. It would be correct to say that David Blackwell was the first Black full professor at UC Berkeley, while Gier became a full professor in 1958.

Joe Gier was born in New Orleans on July 2, 1910, and moved to Oakland as a child. He attended Roosevelt High School where he was an honor roll student involved in various activities. After high school graduation in 1928, he worked briefly before being accepted to UC Berkeley in 1930. He earned his Bachelor of Science degree in Mechanical Engineering in 1933, becoming the earliest known Black student to earn a B.S. in ME from Berkeley. After graduation, he worked as a designing draftsman before returning to Berkeley in 1937 as a laboratory assistant, earning his Master's degree in Engineering in 1940.

Gier formed a professional partnership with ME Prof. Robert Valentine Dunkle in 1943. They helped define the basic concept of using spectral selectivity for efficient photothermal conversion of solar radiation, becoming world experts in thermal and luminous radiation. Gier was listed on eight patents filed between 1948 and 1960, six listing him as primary contributor. Their inventions included the Gier Dunkle Total Hemispherical Radiometer, Infrared Reflectometer, and Black Body Reflectometer. These instruments were used worldwide for heat transfer studies and in America's space laboratories.

Gier began his career at UC Berkeley in 1939 as a lab technician for the California Highway Patrol Illumination Laboratory. He was promoted to Chief Engineer two years later. He was hired as a half-time lecturer in the EE Division in 1946 and was considered 'the best laboratory instructor ever to teach in electrical engineering at Berkeley.' He also supervised the Thermal Radiation Project for the Office of Naval Research. He served as a consultant to various organizations including the US Department of Agriculture and US Army Engineers' Cooperative Snow Survey.

Gier was a very early proponent of solar energy research and energy conservation. He presented a paper at the first-ever international Conference on Solar Energy in 1955. At the time of his death, his research was dedicated almost exclusively to solar energy. He was elected to full membership in Sigma Xi in 1949 and was a member of several professional societies including Eta Kappa Nu and the American Association for the Advancement of Science. He won the APA 'Man of the Year' award in 1950 and was promoted to Full Professor in 1958.

Gier was known for his patience, kindness, and compassion. He took a deep personal interest in his students and was described as 'integrity personified.' He received awards for community service from the APA and Los Angeles Urban League. Shortly after his promotion to full professor in 1958, he transferred to UCLA. He had been suffering from high blood pressure and hoped the move might improve his health. He died on June 22, 1961 at age 50. The Morrin-Gier-Martinelli Heat Transfer Memorial Laboratory at UCLA was partially named in his honor.

Two primary factors contributing to Gier's forgotten legacy were his untimely death in 1961 at age 50 and his transfer from Berkeley to UCLA three years before. Recognition of African American contributions only became part of popular consciousness after the Black Power movement, which peaked a decade after his death. In 1960, solar energy was still considered 'primitive' and wouldn't gain widespread attention until the 1970s energy crisis. Gier's achievements as a scientist, educator, and African American were so singular they appear to have predated a society prepared to recognize them.

Joseph and Kathryn Gier's son, Keith, graduated from Cal Poly with a BS in Mechanical Engineering in 1971 and attended graduate school at UCLA. Following his father's footsteps, Keith specialized in heat transfer and was advised by one of his father's former students. He earned an MSME in 1973 and worked as an aerospace engineer at TRW Defense and Space Systems Group, with another of his father's former students as his mentor. After retiring from Boeing in 2014, Keith has continued working as a contractor at places like NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory and Lockheed Martin.
